stem: A 2-year-old girl presents at 2030 on a Friday with a 24-hour history of a barking cough and a hoarse cry, becoming progressively worse over the evening. She has had a 2-day coryzal illness. Her father reports increasing breathing difficulty over the last hour and audible noise on breathing in. In the resuscitation cubicle she is sitting upright on her father's lap, looks anxious, and is making no attempt to drink. She has continuous inspiratory stridor at rest with marked sternal recession and tracheal tug. There is no drooling, she is afebrile at 37.7 deg C and her SpO2 is 95% on room air, RR 36, HR 142. Her cry is hoarse but she can speak in short phrases. She is fully immunised including Hib vaccine, has no atopy, and there has been no witnessed or suspected foreign body inhalation. There is no other family member unwell. Air entry is symmetrical with no focal crackles or wheeze. Calculated Westley score is 6, placing her in the moderate category. She has had no previous croup admissions or any history of recurrent stridor between viral illnesses. Weight is 13 kg.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of immediate management steps?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg alone, discharge after 1 hour observation if she remains stable
options.rationale: Dexamethasone alone is appropriate for mild croup. With Westley 6, stridor at rest and marked recession, nebulised adrenaline is also required and at least 4 hours of observation post-adrenaline is needed because of rebound (RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, observe 4 hours
options.rationale: Moderate croup (Westley 3 to 7) with stridor at rest and significant work of breathing is treated with nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 plus oral dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg standard, up to 0.6 mg/kg if severe), and observation for at least 4 hours after adrenaline to detect rebound. Adrenaline acts within 30 minutes but lasts only about 2 hours, while steroid onset is around 1 to 2 hours (RCH CPG: Croup; AMH Children's Dosing Companion).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and urgent ENT consult for suspected epiglottitis
options.rationale: Epiglottitis presents with drooling, tripod posture, toxic appearance and muffled (not hoarse) voice in an unimmunised child; this is a typical viral croup picture (Therapeutic Guidelines; RCH CPG: Croup).

options.text: Heliox via face mask and humidified oxygen, holding dexamethasone until the ENT review
options.rationale: Humidified air and heliox have no proven benefit in croup and are not standard of care; withholding dexamethasone is harmful as steroid reduces return visits and admission rates (RCH CPG: Croup; Cochrane).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg, ipratropium 250 microg and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg
options.rationale: This is the treatment for acute asthma, not croup; croup is an upper-airway disease and beta-agonists do not address subglottic oedema (RCH CPG: Croup; National Asthma Council Handbook).

stem: A 10-year-old girl with asthma has been reviewed in the GP clinic with her mother for a routine 3-monthly follow-up. She has been on fluticasone propionate 100 microg twice daily (low-dose ICS) for 8 months. Over the last 3 months she has had daytime wheeze or cough on most days, has woken at night with cough at least once a week, and has needed her salbutamol on average 4 to 5 days per week including before sport. There have been no oral steroid courses in this period and no hospital presentations. The GP has watched her use her MDI and spacer (mouthpiece) in clinic and the technique is excellent. Adherence on Webster pack review is 90% and the parents confirm she takes it morning and night. There are no environmental triggers identified - no smoking in the household, no pets, and no recent changes in housing. Spirometry today shows FEV1 78% predicted and FEV1/FVC 0.74 with significant bronchodilator reversibility of 14%. She is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile and has normal ENT and dermatological examinations. There is no rhinitis, no eczema and no GORD symptoms. She has no allergy or family history requiring specialist input.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in her controller therapy?

options.text: Continue current low-dose ICS and add daily oral montelukast 5 mg as maintenance add-on
options.rationale: Montelukast is an alternative or add-on option but is less effective than adding a LABA at this step in children 6 and over; the National Asthma Council prefers ICS-LABA combination as the preferred step-up (National Asthma Council Handbook).

options.text: Step up to combination ICS plus LABA (fluticasone-salmeterol 100/50 microg twice daily)
options.rationale: She has poor symptom control on low-dose ICS despite correct technique, good adherence and no modifiable factors. The National Asthma Council preferred step-up in children 6 and over is combination low-dose ICS-LABA. Doubling the ICS dose is an alternative but ICS-LABA generally produces better symptom and lung function gains for the same total ICS exposure (National Asthma Council Handbook; GINA 2024).

options.text: Stop the regular ICS and commence as-needed budesonide-formoterol via MDI plus spacer
options.rationale: As-needed ICS-formoterol (the MART approach) is recommended in adolescents and adults at certain steps but is not the preferred maintenance pathway for a 10-year-old who is already on regular ICS with poor control; step-up to combination ICS-LABA is preferred (National Asthma Council Handbook).

options.text: Refer to a paediatric respiratory specialist for assessment for omalizumab or biologic therapy
options.rationale: Biologic therapy is reserved for severe, uncontrolled asthma despite high-dose ICS-LABA plus adherence optimisation, and is a tertiary-care decision; she has not yet had a step-up from low-dose ICS (National Asthma Council Handbook).

options.text: Add a daily long-acting muscarinic antagonist such as tiotropium via Respimat inhaler
options.rationale: LAMA is an add-on for severe asthma uncontrolled on medium- to high-dose ICS-LABA; it is not the first step-up from low-dose ICS in a child (National Asthma Council Handbook).

stem: A 7-week-old previously well boy is brought to ED by his mother after 10 days of a worsening cough. The cough started as a non-specific coryzal illness with mild rhinorrhoea but over the last 5 days has become severely paroxysmal, with episodes of coughing lasting 30 to 60 seconds followed by vomiting and a sharp inspiratory sound. Between paroxysms he is settled and appears well. He has had two witnessed brief apnoeic episodes at home, each lasting less than 15 seconds, with no colour change. He is bottle-fed and taking only about 80% of his usual volume. He is not yet immunised because his mother declined the antenatal pertussis booster and the 6-week vaccines were missed. His mother has had a 3-week non-productive cough that she has attributed to a viral illness. On examination he is afebrile at 36.9 deg C, RR 42, HR 158, SpO2 97% on room air, with mild intercostal recession and unremarkable auscultation between paroxysms. FBC shows white cell count 28 x10^9/L with a marked lymphocytosis. CXR shows perihilar peribronchial thickening with no focal consolidation. A nasopharyngeal pertussis PCR has been sent. He weighs 4.8 kg.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg every 8 hours for 5 days, discharge home with safety-netting
options.rationale: Amoxicillin does not cover Bordetella pertussis; azithromycin is the macrolide of choice. Pertussis in an infant under 6 months with apnoea warrants admission for observation, not discharge, and is notifiable on clinical suspicion (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg daily for 5 days plus salbutamol nebs, no public health action
options.rationale: Azithromycin and admission are appropriate but salbutamol is not effective for pertussis bronchospasm and contact tracing plus household post-exposure prophylaxis is mandatory under CDNA guidelines (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: Admit for monitoring, oral azithromycin for 5 days, notify public health, household chemoprophylaxis
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with pertussis and any apnoeic features should be admitted because of the risk of bradycardia, apnoea and death. Azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 5 days is the first-line macrolide for infants under 6 months (lower risk of pyloric stenosis than erythromycin). Pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion, and close household contacts (especially infants and pregnant women) require post-exposure prophylaxis with the same regimen (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG; NCIRS).

options.text: Oral erythromycin 40 mg/kg/day in 4 doses for 14 days, no admission required
options.rationale: Erythromycin is associated with pyloric stenosis in infants under 1 month and has more GI side effects than azithromycin; admission of an under-6-month-old with apnoea is recommended regardless of SpO2 (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone with oral prednisolone, defer notification until pertussis PCR confirms
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone and prednisolone do not treat pertussis; pertussis is notifiable on clinical suspicion to permit timely contact tracing and prophylaxis, not after laboratory confirmation (CDNA Pertussis SoNG).

stem: An 11-year-old girl is reviewed in the GP clinic 2 weeks after a moderate asthma exacerbation that required oral prednisolone in ED but no admission. She is on fluticasone propionate-salmeterol 100/50 microg twice daily and PRN salbutamol via MDI plus spacer. The clinician has reviewed her technique (excellent) and her adherence (good). The clinician now sits down to write an asthma action plan with the family using the National Asthma Council template. Her mother asks specifically what to do at home when the girl is wheezy and is needing salbutamol more often than usual. Over the last year she has had three episodes that started with several days of increased salbutamol use and mild nocturnal cough before deteriorating - on two occasions her mother gave a single dose of salbutamol and waited overnight before seeking review, and the girl ended up needing oral prednisolone each time. The clinician wants the family to act earlier next time. There is no school attendance issue and the family lives 15 minutes from the nearest ED. The school has a copy of the previous action plan but it is now 18 months old.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice to include in the yellow ('worsening symptoms / use salbutamol more often') zone of her written action plan?

options.text: Continue preventer and use salbutamol 2 puffs every 4 hours, no other action
options.rationale: Yellow zone requires intensification: increased salbutamol dosing (4 to 6 puffs every 4 hours), continuing preventer, considering temporary preventer increase, and arranging GP review within 24 to 48 hours - not waiting for severe deterioration (National Asthma Council Handbook).

options.text: Single oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg at home at first sign of worsening, GP review
options.rationale: Home initiation of oral prednisolone is reserved for selected children with frequent severe exacerbations under specialist guidance and a tailored plan; it is not routine yellow-zone advice in a primary-care action plan (National Asthma Council Handbook).

options.text: Stop the preventer and rely on salbutamol alone until the worsening symptoms resolve
options.rationale: Stopping the preventer worsens control; preventer should be continued or temporarily intensified during a deterioration, not withdrawn (National Asthma Council Handbook).

options.text: Salbutamol 4-6 puffs via spacer every 4 hours, temporarily increase preventer, GP review 24-48 hours
options.rationale: The yellow zone of a National Asthma Council action plan instructs increased salbutamol use (4 to 6 puffs every 4 hours via spacer), continued or temporarily increased preventer, GP review within 24 to 48 hours, and clear red-flag features to escalate to ambulance (no relief from salbutamol, severe breathlessness, blue lips, unable to speak in sentences). Written zone-based plans reduce hospitalisations and time off school (National Asthma Council Handbook; Cochrane Gibson 2003).

options.text: Present immediately to emergency department for any increase in salbutamol use regardless of severity
options.rationale: Routine ED presentation for minor increases in salbutamol use leads to unnecessary attendances and undermines self-management; clear thresholds for ED versus GP versus home intensification are the purpose of the action plan (National Asthma Council Handbook).

stem: A 12-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis (homozygous F508del) is admitted with a 7-day history of worsening productive cough, increased sputum volume that has become darker green and more tenacious than her usual baseline, reduced exercise tolerance with breathlessness climbing one flight of stairs at school, anorexia and 1.5 kg of weight loss over the previous 2 weeks despite her usual enzyme dosing. Her baseline FEV1 is 78% predicted; today it has fallen to 61% predicted on bedside spirometry, the largest single-visit drop she has had since starting her current modulator. She is fully adherent to her usual regimen of nebulised hypertonic saline 6%, dornase alfa, pancreatic enzyme replacement and twice-daily airway clearance physiotherapy with a positive expiratory pressure device. She is on elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor and has been stable for the last 18 months. She is fully immunised including annual influenza and is up to date with the seasonal RSV programme. Her last sputum culture 6 weeks ago grew mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa sensitive to tobramycin, ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, and meropenem, and methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus sensitive to flucloxacillin. She has no penicillin allergy and her baseline serum creatinine 6 weeks ago was 55 micromol/L. On examination she is alert, T 37.8 deg C, RR 26, HR 110, SpO2 93% on room air. Auscultation reveals coarse crackles at both upper lobes and the right middle lobe with widespread expiratory wheeze. CXR shows bilateral upper-lobe bronchiectasis with new patchy consolidation at the right middle lobe compared to her baseline. A sputum culture has been sent on arrival. Her weight is 38 kg.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate empirical IV antibiotic regimen pending fresh sputum culture results?

options.text: Oral ciprofloxacin 20 mg/kg twice daily for 14 days as outpatient antipseudomonal monotherapy with home physiotherapy
options.rationale: Oral ciprofloxacin monotherapy is used for early Pseudomonas eradication or mild outpatient exacerbations; an FEV1 drop of 17 percentage points with new consolidation, weight loss and hypoxia warrants IV dual therapy with admission (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily plus oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone does not reliably cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa; ceftazidime, piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime or meropenem is required. Azithromycin has anti-inflammatory effects in CF but is not adequate antipseudomonal therapy (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus oral rifampicin as targeted antistaphylococcal combination therapy
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin targets MSSA, which is reasonable additional cover but does not address the chronic Pseudomonas that is far more likely to be driving this exacerbation given her sputum history (Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Pulmonary Guidelines).

options.text: IV meropenem 40 mg/kg every 8 hours as broad-spectrum antipseudomonal monotherapy for 14 days
options.rationale: Carbapenem monotherapy is reserved for multi-resistant Pseudomonas or beta-lactam allergy; standard practice in a child with sensitive mucoid Pseudomonas is a beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside combination, preserving carbapenems for resistant strains (Therapeutic Guidelines; CF Australia Standards of Care).

options.text: IV ceftazidime 50 mg/kg every 8 hours plus IV tobramycin 10 mg/kg once daily, alongside intensified airway clearance physiotherapy
options.rationale: A CF pulmonary exacerbation in an adolescent with chronic mucoid Pseudomonas is treated empirically with dual anti-pseudomonal IV therapy combining an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (commonly ceftazidime) with an aminoglycoside (tobramycin once daily, with levels monitored) for 10 to 14 days. Intensified airway clearance physiotherapy and nutritional optimisation are essential adjuncts and her CFTR modulator is continued. MSSA is co-covered or treated separately if persistent (Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory; CF Australia Standards of Care; Cystic Fibrosis Foundation Guidelines).

stem: A 10-year-old boy, weight 32 kg, presents to ED 25 minutes after a wasp sting with urticaria, lip swelling, stridor, expiratory wheeze and persistent hypotension. Paramedics gave IM adrenaline 300 microg en route. On arrival to ED 10 minutes later he remained tachypnoeic with stridor, wheeze and BP 78/40, so a second IM adrenaline 300 microg was given into the anterolateral thigh and an IV 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus was commenced. Now 5 minutes after the second IM dose he remains drowsy with BP 82/44, HR 156, RR 40, SpO2 92% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, with audible stridor and wheeze. Both IM doses were confirmed delivered correctly. The ED consultant is at the bedside, anaesthetics has been called for airway support, and a second IV line has been sited. His past medical history is unremarkable, he takes no regular medications, he has no known allergies and his immunisations are up to date. There is no family history of allergic disease, asthma, or cardiac disorders. He had been camping with his family at a regional park and was helping clear food scraps from the picnic table when stung on the right forearm. He has no relevant drug allergies, no recent fasting, and a full set of bloods including tryptase has been sent.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Bolus IV adrenaline 1 mg (1:10,000) over 1 minute
options.rationale: A 1 mg IV bolus is the cardiac arrest dose and would likely cause hypertensive crisis, myocardial ischaemia or arrhythmia in a perfusing patient; infusion is preferred (ASCIA).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and IV promethazine 0.25 mg/kg
options.rationale: Corticosteroids and antihistamines do not treat refractory anaphylaxis and promethazine is contraindicated under 2 years and can worsen hypotension; adrenaline infusion is required (ASCIA).

options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 0.1 microg/kg/min, titrated to response
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis after two correctly delivered IM doses is an indication for IV adrenaline infusion, starting at 0.1 microg/kg/min (range 0.05-1 microg/kg/min) and titrated to BP, perfusion and airway response, while continuing fluid resuscitation. Boluses of IV adrenaline outside cardiac arrest carry significant risk of arrhythmia and hypertensive crisis and are not first-line (ASCIA Refractory Anaphylaxis Guidelines).

options.text: Continue IM adrenaline every 5 minutes only and avoid IV adrenaline
options.rationale: After two failed IM doses, IM absorption is unreliable in shock; IV infusion is indicated (ASCIA Refractory Guidelines).

options.text: Commence IV glucagon 30 microg/kg as first-line, no further adrenaline
options.rationale: Glucagon is considered only in patients on beta-blockers with refractory shock; it does not replace adrenaline infusion (ASCIA).

stem: A 6-year-old girl, weight 22 kg, was brought in shocked with presumed septic source (community-acquired pneumonia with a parapneumonic effusion on bedside lung ultrasound). She received empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg early in the resuscitation. Over the last 40 minutes she has received three 20 mL/kg boluses of 0.9% saline (total 60 mL/kg) and remains tachycardic at 156, with central capillary refill of 4 seconds, cool mottled peripheries, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 38, SpO2 94% on 8 L Hudson mask. She has not voided since arrival. The hepatic edge is now palpable 1 cm below the costal margin and there are bibasal crackles. Lactate has fallen from 5.4 to 3.8 mmol/L. The retrieval service is on the phone and the paediatric registrar is preparing for central access. The child has no congenital heart disease. She has no known drug allergies and her immunisations including 13-valent pneumococcal and influenza are up to date. Her mother reports a 3-day febrile illness with cough and lethargy treated by the GP with oral amoxicillin 48 hours ago without improvement. There is no chronic medical condition, no recent travel, no immunocompromise and no contact with an unwell sibling. The bedside echocardiogram clip the registrar has just performed shows a hyperdynamic but underfilled left ventricle with no pericardial effusion.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence peripheral adrenaline infusion 0.05-0.1 microg/kg/min and prepare for intubation
options.rationale: She has fluid-refractory septic shock with cold-shock physiology (narrow pulse pressure, mottled cool peripheries, prolonged CR) and now signs of fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles). Australian guidelines support starting adrenaline as first-line inotrope in paediatric cold septic shock, and peripheral infusion at low-dose concentrations (0.05-0.3 microg/kg/min) is acceptable while central access is obtained. Further large-volume fluid boluses risk worsening overload (ANZICS Paediatric Sepsis; APLS; Surviving Sepsis Campaign paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give a fourth 20 mL/kg saline bolus before any vasoactive medication
options.rationale: With hepatomegaly and crackles signalling overload, further large boluses are likely to worsen oedema and hypoxaemia; inotrope is the next step (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

options.text: Start noradrenaline as first-line inotrope at 0.1 microg/kg/min peripherally
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is preferred in warm shock with wide pulse pressure and vasodilation; in cold shock with poor cardiac output, adrenaline is first-line in paediatric guidelines (APLS).

options.text: Commence dopamine infusion 10 microg/kg/min peripherally as first-line
options.rationale: Dopamine has fallen out of favour as first-line in paediatric septic shock due to inferior outcomes vs adrenaline/noradrenaline (Ventura 2015 RCT; Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg as next step, no inotrope
options.rationale: Stress-dose hydrocortisone is an adjunct in catecholamine-resistant shock or suspected adrenal insufficiency, not a substitute for inotropic support in fluid-refractory shock (Surviving Sepsis paediatric 2020).

stem: A 4-month-old, ex-term, breastfed boy is brought to ED by his mother because of unsettled crying for the last 12 hours. She mentions that she noticed two bruises on his cheek and a bruise on his left ear earlier in the day, which she attributes to him knocking himself on a toy in the cot. He has not yet rolled or crawled and is not pulling himself up. There is no preceding illness and no fever. He is fully immunised and has had routine well-child checks with no concerns. Examination reveals a well-grown alert infant with a 2 cm bruise to the right cheek, a 1 cm bruise to the left pinna and a 1.5 cm bruise on the left frontal scalp, no other external injuries, a normal anterior fontanelle, no focal neurology, no retinal haemorrhages on examination by the registrar, normal tone, and a normal abdomen with no organomegaly. Coagulation screen, FBC and LFTs are normal. The mother is alone and tearful when asked about who else cares for the baby. There is no family history of bleeding disorder, no preceding viral illness, and no recent immunisation in the last 7 days.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Send for outpatient coagulation studies only and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Coags are already normal and a bleeding disorder does not explain pre-mobile bruising in suspicious patterns; comprehensive abuse workup and notification are required (RCH CPG).

options.text: Reassure the mother and discharge with GP follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Bruises in a pre-mobile infant are never normal - 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise'. Discharge without investigation and mandatory reporting is a serious safeguarding failure (RCH CPG).

options.text: Admit for skeletal survey, CT head, ophthalmology review, child protection consult and mandatory report
options.rationale: Bruising in a pre-mobile infant meets the TEN-4 FACESp rule (Torso, Ear, Neck, age <=4 months; Frenulum, Angle of jaw, Cheek, Eyelid, Sclera, patterned) and is a strong red flag for inflicted injury. This child has all four red-flag locations triggered. Mandatory next steps are full skeletal survey (and repeat at 2 weeks), non-contrast CT head, dilated ophthalmology examination for retinal haemorrhages, paediatric team and child protection consultation, and mandatory notification to the state child protection authority. Doctors are mandated reporters in every Australian state and territory (RCH CPG Child abuse; AAP TEN-4 FACES).

options.text: Order a skeletal survey only, without notifying child protection, pending the imaging result
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting is triggered by reasonable suspicion, not by waiting for confirmatory imaging; delay risks further harm and breaches statutory duty (Children and Young Persons Acts; RCH CPG).

options.text: Confront the mother directly about possible abuse before doing any investigations
options.rationale: Confrontation is not the role of the treating clinician; the priority is the child's safety, the workup, multidisciplinary consultation and statutory notification (RCH CPG).

stem: A 4-year-old girl, weight 16 kg, was pulled from a backyard pool by her father approximately 6 minutes ago after an unwitnessed submersion estimated at 3-5 minutes. She was apnoeic and unresponsive at poolside. Her father began chest compressions immediately and bystander CPR continued until paramedics arrived 4 minutes ago. Paramedics found her in asystole, established a supraglottic airway, ventilated with 100% oxygen and continued compressions; ROSC was achieved after 3 minutes of resuscitation. She arrives in ED still intubated (now endotracheal), with HR 132, BP 86/52, SpO2 92% on 100% FiO2, T 33.4 degC tympanic, GCS 3 with no sedation, pupils 4 mm sluggish bilaterally. Bedside lung ultrasound shows bilateral B-lines and the CXR is in progress. The pool was uncovered and the temperature ambient. There was no chlorine exposure or chemical contamination of the water. She had no known medical conditions, no medications, no allergies, and her immunisations are up to date. There is no family history of arrhythmia, sudden cardiac death, long QT syndrome, or epilepsy that might have preceded the submersion. Her father witnessed the entire event from the kitchen window and estimates the maximum time submerged at 5 minutes. An indwelling urinary catheter has drained 20 mL of clear urine since insertion 15 minutes ago, and the PICU retrieval team has been activated.
lead_in: Which best reflects current ANZCOR-aligned management priorities for this child?

options.text: Withdraw care now given asystole at scene; outcome is uniformly poor
options.rationale: Paediatric drowning with ROSC and short submersion time can have favourable neurological outcomes; early prognostication is unreliable and resuscitation should continue with PICU input (ANZCOR).

options.text: Hyperventilate to PaCO2 25 mmHg and target SpO2 100% on FiO2 1.0
options.rationale: Hyperoxia and hypocapnia worsen post-arrest neurological outcomes through cerebral vasoconstriction and oxidative injury; target normoxia and normocapnia (ANZCOR).

options.text: Aggressive rapid rewarming to 38 degC plus prophylactic broad-spectrum IV ceftriaxone now
options.rationale: Rapid rewarming and induced hyperthermia worsen neurological outcomes; antibiotics are reserved for evidence of infection or contaminated water exposure with specific organisms (ANZCOR).

options.text: Early extubation because the lungs are clear on auscultation and CXR is unremarkable
options.rationale: Pulmonary oedema and ARDS from drowning can be delayed (up to 8 hours); the patient has bilateral B-lines and a low GCS, both contraindicating early extubation (ANZCOR).

options.text: Lung-protective ventilation with PEEP, controlled rewarming to 36 degC, post-arrest care, discuss ECMO retrieval
options.rationale: Post-drowning care follows ANZCOR resuscitation principles plus post-cardiac-arrest care. Five initial rescue breaths precede compressions in drowning because hypoxia is the proximate cause. After ROSC, target normoxia (SpO2 94-98%), normocapnia, lung-protective ventilation with PEEP for pulmonary oedema/ARDS, treat shock with fluids and adrenaline, and discuss VA-ECMO for refractory hypoxia or cardiac dysfunction. Rewarm to 36 degC at 0.5-1 degC/h (do not over-rewarm; mild controlled hypothermia is no longer routinely targeted but normothermia is). Antibiotics are not routine.

stem: A 15-year-old girl, weight 60 kg, is brought to ED by her mother 5 hours after ingesting an unknown number of paracetamol 500 mg tablets in an acute single ingestion. She is now tearful but cooperative, denies co-ingestion, has no abdominal pain, no jaundice, and a soft non-tender abdomen. She has no chronic liver disease and is not on enzyme-inducing medications. Vital signs are normal. Bloods drawn 4 hours and 30 minutes post-ingestion show paracetamol concentration 165 mg/L, ALT 28 U/L, INR 1.0, creatinine 58 micromol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, glucose 5.0 mmol/L, and a negative beta-hCG. The empty packet retrieved at home suggests she may have taken approximately 14 g (around 230 mg/kg) but the ingestion history is uncertain. The Australian paracetamol nomogram has a single treatment line starting at 150 mg/L at 4 hours, dropping to 75 mg/L at 8 hours and 37 mg/L at 12 hours, after which the line is extended to 24 hours. She has no history of self-harm, no current psychiatric medications, no chronic medical illness and no regular paracetamol or hepatic enzyme-inducing medications. She lives at home with her parents, attends Year 10 and has been struggling academically and socially over the past 2 months. Her mother brought her in immediately on finding the empty packet and a written note in her bedroom. The CAMHS on-call clinician has been contacted, the empty Panadol packet has been retained for documentation, and the toxicology registrar has reviewed the bloods and the timing of the level.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat paracetamol level at 8 hours and only commence NAC if above 75 mg/L
options.rationale: If the 4-hour-equivalent level is already above the treatment line, NAC should be commenced immediately rather than waiting; delaying NAC beyond 8 hours significantly reduces its hepatoprotective efficacy (Australian PIC).

options.text: IV N-acetylcysteine two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) and admit
options.rationale: At 4.5 hours post-ingestion the paracetamol concentration of 165 mg/L lies above the Australian 150 mg/L treatment line on the modified Rumack-Matthew nomogram (extrapolated back to 4 h would be above 150 mg/L). The reported dose also exceeds the 200 mg/kg supratherapeutic threshold. NAC is indicated. The Australian Poisons Information Centre and AMH endorse the two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg over 16 h) which reduces anaphylactoid reactions versus the traditional three-bag regimen. Psychiatric assessment is required after any deliberate self-poisoning (AMH; Australian PIC; Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology and Wilderness).

options.text: Give oral activated charcoal 1 g/kg now and reassess level at 12 hours
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective within 1-2 hours of ingestion (up to 4 hours for sustained release); at 5 hours post-ingestion it is unlikely to be useful, and NAC should not be delayed (Australian PIC).

options.text: Discharge with mental health follow-up only; level is below treatment line and dose under 200 mg/kg
options.rationale: 165 mg/L at 4.5 hours is above the 150 mg/L treatment line, and the reported dose of approximately 230 mg/kg exceeds 200 mg/kg; NAC is indicated. Even if there is uncertainty, the higher of the two assessment methods (level vs dose) directs management (Australian PIC).

options.text: Commence oral methionine and arrange urgent liver transplant assessment at tertiary centre now
options.rationale: IV NAC is the standard antidote in Australia; oral methionine is not used. Transplant assessment is reserved for established fulminant hepatic failure (King's College criteria) which is not present here (AMH).
